title: Paste paper with iron oxide pigment
subtitle: Combing and finger-drawing patterns in wheat paste coloured with earth pigments
excerpt: Paste paper, paper coated in tinted wheat paste and patterned while wet, was widely used as a decorated bookbinding paper in seventeenth-century Germany. The surface is worked with combs, fingers, and brushes to create repeating textures in the wet paste, then dried flat. Iron oxide earth pigments give warm, lightfast colours. The result is a flexible, durable decorative paper.
sourceNotes: The German paste paper tradition is documented from the seventeenth century in court bookbinding records. Richard Wolfe, Marbled Paper: Its History, Techniques and Patterns (1990) discusses the technique in the context of European decorated paper traditions. The flour-paste base recipe has been in continuous use and is described in numerous pre-1928 craft sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: Decorated paper made by coating a sheet with a starch paste and working patterns into the surface while wet. A seventeenth-century German bookbinding tradition. The paste is usually tinted with pigment and combed, printed, or finger-drawn.

Naturally occurring iron-oxide based pigments, ochre, umber, sienna, and red oxide, ground from clay minerals. Lightfast, non-toxic, and inexpensive; the standard pigments in traditional paste paper work.
A paste paper pattern made by pressing a textured object (a crumpled cloth, a coiled rope, a comb) into the wet paste and lifting it away, leaving an impression.

content.text: is one of the simplest decorated paper techniques to learn and one of the most variable in its results. The same basic paste-and-comb approach produces everything from tight repeating wave patterns to loose, hand-drawn landscapes depending on how the wet surface is worked. The
, iron oxides, are the most reliable choice: they mix evenly into paste, do not bleed, and hold their warm reddish-brown tones when dry.

items.name: Wheat paste (cooked from flour and water, or commercial pre-cooked paste)
items.substitutions: Rice paste; cornflour paste, similar working time

items.name: Iron oxide earth pigment, red, yellow ochre, or raw umber
items.qty: 1 to 2 teaspoons per 100 ml of paste
items.substitutions: Artist's acrylic paint mixed into the paste; food colouring (less lightfast)

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, pre-dampened slightly
items.substitutions: Smooth hot-press watercolour paper; thick cartridge absorbs the moisture in the paste better than thin stock

content.text: Mix dry iron oxide pigment into a small amount of cold water to form a smooth slurry. Add this to the wheat paste and stir thoroughly until evenly tinted. The working consistency should be like thick single cream, spreadable but not runny.
Test the colour on a scrap sheet, paste paper colours dry lighter than they appear when wet. Adjust the pigment quantity as needed.
Wet the paper sheet lightly on both sides with a damp sponge and tape it to a flat, non-absorbent board. This prevents the paper from cockling when the paste is applied.
Spread the tinted paste over the entire sheet surface with a wide brush or palette knife, working from the centre outward. The coat should be even but not thick, approximately 1 mm.

While still wet, work patterns into the paste surface. A marbling comb pulled across the paste in parallel waves gives a classic scallop pattern. Fingers drawn through give freer, more organic marks. A
with a crumpled cloth gives a crinkled texture.
Allow to dry flat and undisturbed. The paste shrinks slightly as it dries; if the paper was not taped down it will cockle. Remove the tape once fully dry.

items.symptom: The paste cracks as it dries
items.cause: The layer was too thick, or the paste had too little water.
items.fix: Apply a thinner coat. If cracks appear, re-wet the surface lightly and smooth with a damp brush before the paste sets fully.

items.symptom: Comb marks close up before the paste dries
items.cause: The paste is too fluid; working time is short.
items.fix: Thicken the paste by stirring in a little extra flour. Work the pattern quickly after spreading.
